description: Major standing naval deployments per decade, 1650-1730. 'ships' = approximate ships-of-war (rated ships / galleys) normally on station; honest approximations after N.A.M. Rodger, The Command of the Ocean, and Jan Glete, Navies and Nations. Coordinates [lat/lon] WGS84 mark the station's usual cruising centre or base.

stations.note: Blake's and Monck's Commonwealth fleet in the First Anglo-Dutch War 1652-54 (Rodger).
Blake bombards Porto Farina 1655 and burns the plate fleet at Santa Cruz de Tenerife 1657.
Penn and Venables fail at Santo Domingo, take Jamaica 1655.
Tromp falls at Scheveningen 1653; convoy defence is the Dutch raison d'etre (Glete).
De Ruyter convoys Smyrna traders past Barbary cruisers.
Battered guard of the Andalusian coast and flota landfalls.
Veracruz-Havana silver run; capitana and almiranta plus pataches.
Cartagena and Portobelo fairs; Potosi silver embarked at Panama.
Carries Potosi silver Arica-Callao-Panama; unchallenged until the buccaneers come overland.
The Fronde leaves the French navy derelict; revival awaits Colbert.
Armed sugar convoys Lisbon-Bahia-Recife, instituted 1649 against the Dutch.
Sailing corsairs range past Gibraltar to Biscay and beyond; captives to the bagnios.
Small fast rovers off the Atlantic approaches to the Strait.
Galleys and galleons fight Venice at the Dardanelles, 1654-57.
Second Anglo-Dutch War 1665-67: Lowestoft, Four Days Battle; the Medway disgrace 1667.
Tangier (dowry, 1662) gives England a Strait base; Lawson treats with Algiers.
Modyford instead commissions buccaneers — Myngs, then Morgan — as cheap sea power.
De Ruyter's fleet at its height (Rodger; Glete).
Indiamen mass at the Cape and run home north-about Scotland in wartime.
Re-established 1665 at Veracruz to sweep the Windward Passage of buccaneers; rarely succeeds.
Havana rendezvous; sailings grow irregular as Seville's trade decays (Parry).
Pacific silver escort, unchanged routine.
Colbert's construction programme begins 1661; arsenals at Brest and Rochefort.
Ships and galleys against Barbary; Jijelli expedition 1664 fails.
Sugar convoys continue under the Junta do Comércio.
War with England 1668-71; Allin and Spragge bombard.
Supply of the Candia siege until the city falls, 1669.
Third Anglo-Dutch War 1672-74: Solebay, Schooneveld, Texel — all frustrated by de Ruyter.
Narbrough's and later Herbert's cruisers force the 1682 Algiers treaty.
After Morgan sacks Panama (1671) policy turns: hunt the buccaneers instead.
De Ruyter dies off Sicily 1676 fighting Duquesne.
Evertsen and Binckes retake New York 1673; Binckes dies at Tobago 1677.
The young Royale now outbuilds all rivals on paper (Glete).
Duquesne beats the Dutch-Spanish fleets at Stromboli and Augusta, 1676.
Takes Tobago 1678; wrecks almost entire on the Aves reef the same year.
Too few hulls to stop Morgan; reconstituted again 1677.
Annual frota; escorts thin in peacetime.
At war with England to 1682, then with France.
Tangier evacuated 1684; Herbert's cruisers keep the Algiers peace.
Peacetime economy under Charles II and James II; the fleet refits for the coming French war.
Frigates now hang ex-buccaneers; Henry Morgan, Lieutenant-Governor, helps.
Duquesne's bomb-vessels shatter Algiers 1682-83; Genoa bombed 1684.
By 1690 the Royale is briefly the largest navy afloat (Glete).
Economy after 1678; the 1688 descent on England is its last great solo effort.
Chases the pirates who sack Veracruz (1683) and Campeche (1685), mostly in vain.
Skirmishes with South Sea buccaneers off Panama 1685.
Rio grows as the southern terminus; Sacramento supplied against Spanish pressure.
Humbled by French bombs, the regency turns to ransom diplomacy.
Defends the Archipelago against Venice after 1684.
Beachy Head lost 1690, Barfleur-La Hogue won 1692 (Rodger).
Russell winters at Cádiz 1694-95 — the first English fleet kept out all winter.
A regular station from the 1690s; yellow fever kills crews faster than the French.
Wright's and Wheler's squadrons trade island raids with the French.
Guardships at Boston and New York; Phips's 1690 Quebec attempt fails.
Annual convoy and commodore for the fishery; Plaisance French raids answered.
Tourville's fleet; after 1694 starved of funds in favour of privateers.
Jean Bart and the guerre de course devour Allied convoys (Rodger).
Watches Spain and the Allied Mediterranean fleet.
Pointis and Ducasse, with buccaneers, sack Cartagena 1697.
Treaty quota: three Dutch to five English ships.
Cannot prevent the loss of Cartagena's treasure 1697.
First gold remittances from Minas Gerais ride the frota.
Rooke takes the Vigo plate-fleet prizes 1702, Gibraltar 1704.
Málaga 1704; Shovell lost on the Scillies coming home 1707.
Benbow's last fight off Santa Marta 1702; two captains shot for cowardice.
Wager's successors convoy the sugar trade; Walker repulsed at Guadeloupe 1703.
Convoys and coast guard from Boston to Virginia against French privateers.
Fishery convoy continues through the war.
Château-Renault convoys the silver to Vigo, 1702 — and loses the escort there.
Scuttled in harbour during the Allied siege, 1707; never recovers.
Ducasse convoys Spanish silver; fights Benbow 1702, Wager's prey 1708.
San José blows up off Barú 1708 with her treasure — Wager's Action.
Skeleton force; French squadrons do the real escorting.
Last war as a first-rank navy; finances exhausted by 1713 (Glete).
Allied from 1703 (Methuen); gold fleets sail under English protection.
Norris's Baltic squadrons (from 1715) shepherd naval-stores trade past the Great Northern War.
Minorca held from 1708; Byng annihilates the new Spanish fleet off Cape Passaro, 1718.
Turns from French privateers to pirate suppression after 1713.
Sloops and sixth-rates against the post-war pirate flood.
Walker's Quebec expedition wrecks on Egg Island 1711; later HMS Pearl and Lyme hunt pirates.
Rogers re-establishes crown rule at Nassau 1718; the King's Pardon scatters the pirates.
Routine resumes; Utrecht gives Britain the whole shore.
Post-war collapse; the Regency keeps only a cadre fleet.
Plague of 1720 and penury keep it in port.
Patiño's revived navy lands armies on Sardinia and Sicily 1717-18; destroyed at Cape Passaro.
The 1715 plate fleet wrecks on Florida; salvage camps draw every pirate in the Indies.
Reduced to trade protection; the Republic retires from fleet rivalry.
Rio convoys now the richest sailings on earth; pirates haunt the Guinea leg.
Deters Russia in the last years of the Northern War, 1720-21.
Walpole's fleet-in-being; mobilized for the 1726-27 crises.
Port Mahon and Gibraltar; covers the 1727 Spanish siege of Gibraltar.
Sloops sweep the last pirate crews; gallows busy at Port Royal and Kingston.
Chaloner Ogle kills Bartholomew Roberts off Cape Lopez, Feb 1722; 52 hanged at Cape Coast.
Greyhound takes Charles Harris's crew 1723; 26 hanged at Newport.
Hosier's blockade of Portobelo 1726-27: the treasure stays, 4,000 men die of fever.
Private commissioned cruisers seize British traders — the grievances of the coming Jenkins' Ear war.
Patiño rebuilds steadily; flotas sail again on a regular footing.
Compagnie des Indes escorts to the Mascarenes and Pondicherry.
Cape squadron warned for pirates after the 1722 Taylor scare.
The Lisbon-Rio run at its richest; escorted men-of-war carry the king's fifth.
Still cruising, but bound by treaty to spare British and French flags.
The peacetime reserve of the world's largest navy (Rodger).
Port Mahon routine; consuls keep the Barbary treaties.
Now chiefly against guarda-costas and smugglers; piracy effectively extinct.
Sugar-convoy guardships.
Guardships at Boston and New York.
A respectable second-rank fleet again under Patiño (Glete).
Ear-cropping seizures continue; war comes in 1739.
Maurepas begins the revival that will contest the next war.
Levant trade protection.
A trade-protection navy only.
Bahia and Rio convoys under men-of-war.
Cruises against Italian and Iberian small craft; the great raids are memory.
